<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-krug" color="red" summary="UX principles for upcoming features">
Hey Marty, Steve Krug here. I've been looking at the upcoming features (Tasks 4-9) and I want to share my UX perspective before we start building. These are the things I care about most from a usability standpoint:

**Registration (Task 4):**
- Two fields: email and password. That's it. No name, no username, no confirm password (let them see their password with a toggle instead). Every extra field is a reason to quit.
- One button: "Create account." Not "Submit." Not "Register." Action words.
- Error messages in plain English right next to the field that has the problem. "That email is already registered — want to sign in instead?" with a link to the login page.
- After successful registration, drop them straight into their (empty) todo list. No confirmation page, no "welcome to the app" screen.

**Login (Task 5):**
- Email, password, "Sign in" button. That's the whole page.
- "Forgot password" link (even if we don't build it for MVP — the link can go to a "coming soon" page). Users expect to see it.
- Wrong password? "That email or password didn't work. Try again." Don't tell them which one is wrong (security) but keep the language friendly.
- After login: straight to the todo list.

**The Todo List (Tasks 6-9):**
- After login, the user sees their list. No dashboard, no sidebar, no settings gear. Just the list.
- Add-a-todo input is always visible at the top. Single text field. Type, press Enter. The new item appears in the list. The input clears and stays focused. Done.
- Empty state: "No todos yet." with the input field right there, obvious. Maybe even a subtle prompt like placeholder text "What do you need to do?"
- Completing a todo: checkbox. One click. Satisfying visual feedback — strikethrough, maybe a subtle fade.
- Deleting: a small delete button/icon on each item. Maybe confirm for the first time, but honestly, for a todo list? Just delete it. Undo is better than confirm.
- Editing: click on the text, it becomes editable. Press Enter or click away to save. Escape to cancel. No edit button, no modal.

**The trunk test — at every screen, the user should know:**
- Where am I? (Clear page title/heading)
- What can I do? (Obvious actions)
- How do I get out? (Sign out link, always visible)

Would love to see your user stories align with these principles. The simpler we keep this, the better it will work. Every screen should pass the "would my mom know what to do here?" test.
</teammate-message>

content.text:
Good input, Steve. Let me review your suggestions against the user stories and my product decisions. Some of these I agree with strongly, a couple need discussion.

Let me re-read the current user stories to compare specifics.

   287→- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
   288→- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
   289→- Very long replacement text
   290→- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
   291→- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)
   292→
   293→### Success Metric
   294→
   295→- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
   296→  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.
   297→
   298→---
   299→
   300→## Priority and Implementation Order
   301→
   302→Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:
   303→
   304→1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
   305→2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
   306→3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
   307→4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
   308→5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
   309→6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
   310→7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
   311→8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
   312→9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
   313→10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)
   314→
   315→This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
   316→8-10 are enhancements on the core.
   317→
   318→---
   319→
   320→## Product Success Criteria (MVP)
   321→
   322→The MVP is successful when:
   323→
   324→1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
   325→2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
   326→3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   327→4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
   328→5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
   329→6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD
   330→
   331→We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
   332→add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.

# User Stories -- MVP

These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.

Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
end-to-end first, then layer on features.

---

## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)

**As a** new user,
**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
  (user is automatically logged in)
- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match
- [ ] If email is already registered, show a clear error message (do NOT reveal whether
  the email exists in the system for security -- use a generic message like "Unable to
  create account. Please check your details and try again.")
- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
- Concurrent registration with the same email
- Empty form submission
- Whitespace-only inputs
- Password exactly at minimum length

### Success Metric

- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.

---

## US-2: User Login (Must Have)

**As a** registered user,
**I want to** log in with my email and password,
**So that** I can access my personal todo list.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
- [ ] Invalid credentials show a generic error: "Invalid email or password" (do NOT
  reveal which field is wrong)
- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- User who has not registered tries to log in
- Correct email, wrong password
- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)

### Success Metric

- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.

---

## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** log out of my account,
**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Double-clicking logout
- Logout when session has already expired
- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)

### Success Metric

- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.

---

## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
**So that** I know what I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`)
- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a friendly, helpful message
  (e.g., "No todos yet. Add one above to get started.") -- not a blank screen
- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
- [ ] Page works without JavaScript

### Edge Cases

- User with zero todos (empty state)
- User with one todo
- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)

### Success Metric

- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.

---

## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
**So that** I can track a task I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo")
- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
  next item)
- [ ] Empty title submission is rejected with a clear inline error
- [ ] Title has a maximum length (TBD by team -- suggested 200 characters)
- [ ] Whitespace-only titles are rejected
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
  HTMX swap

### Edge Cases

- Adding an item with only whitespace
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has an edit mechanism (e.g., clicking the title, an edit button)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
  focus returns to the item
- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
- Very long replacement text
- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)

### Success Metric

- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

---

## Priority and Implementation Order

Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:

1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)

This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
8-10 are enhancements on the core.

---

## Product Success Criteria (MVP)

The MVP is successful when:

1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD

We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.

content.text: Good, I've reviewed the stories against Steve's suggestions. Let me respond with my product analysis.

input.content:
Steve, great input. I love most of this. Let me go through each point and tell you where we align, where I'm updating the stories, and where I want the mob to weigh in.

**Registration -- where we agree:**
- "Create account" button label -- yes, absolutely. Action words. I'll update the story.
- Drop them straight into the todo list -- already in the acceptance criteria. No confirmation page.
- Error messages in plain English next to the field -- already there.

**Registration -- where I'm pushing back or need the mob:**
- **Dropping password confirmation**: I hear you on "every extra field is a reason to quit." But I want Heydon and the mob to weigh in. The password visibility toggle is a good alternative -- it's arguably better UX because you can *see* what you typed. But it requires JavaScript for the toggle (progressive enhancement concern -- without JS, you can't toggle visibility, and you're back to typing blind with no confirmation). Let's bring this to the mob. My lean: keep the confirm field for the no-JS baseline, add a visibility toggle as a progressive enhancement that hides the confirm field when JS is available.
- **Revealing whether an email exists**: Your suggestion of "That email is already registered -- want to sign in instead?" is friendly but it's an account enumeration vulnerability. An attacker can probe emails to see who has accounts. The current story uses a generic message deliberately. Security wins here. We can still link to the sign-in page from the error, just with generic wording: "Unable to create account. If you already have an account, try signing in." Same helpfulness, no information leak.

**Login -- where we agree:**
- Email, password, "Sign in" -- already aligned.
- Generic error message -- already in the criteria.
- Straight to the todo list -- already there.

**Login -- "Forgot password" link:**
This is a good UX call. Users expect to see it. But building password reset is absolutely out of scope for MVP (it requires email sending infrastructure, token generation, expiry, etc.). Your suggestion of a "coming soon" page is pragmatic. I'll add it to the story as: "Forgot password link is present but leads to a placeholder page explaining the feature is coming soon." It sets the right expectation without bloating scope.

**Todo list -- where we agree strongly:**
- No dashboard, no sidebar, no settings gear. Just the list. 100%.
- Add input always visible at the top. Type, Enter, done. Already in the criteria.
- Empty state with the input field right there. Already there. I like your placeholder text idea -- "What do you need to do?" is good. I'll add that.
- Checkbox for completion. One click. Strikethrough. Already aligned.

**Delete -- where I want to discuss:**
- You're saying skip the confirmation, just delete it. I understand the argument -- it's a todo list, not a bank transfer. But "undo is better than confirm" means building an undo mechanism, which is more complex than a lightweight inline confirm. For MVP, I think a small inline confirm (not a modal, not an alert -- just replacing the delete button with "Confirm? Yes / No" inline) is the right tradeoff. Quick, safe, no extra infrastructure. If the mob disagrees, I'll listen, but undo goes to `docs/future-ideas.md`.

**Edit -- where we agree:**
- Click to edit inline, Enter to save, Escape to cancel. Already in the criteria. The no-JS fallback navigates to a separate edit page (progressive enhancement requirement).

I'm going to update the user stories now with the changes we agree on. The password confirm vs. toggle question goes to the mob.

Summary of updates I'm making:
1. Button labels: "Create account" for registration, "Sign in" for login
2. Add "Forgot password?" placeholder link to login
3. Add placeholder text suggestion for the add-todo input
4. Tighten the registration error copy while keeping it secure
5. Note the password confirm vs. toggle as an open question for the mob

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# User Stories -- MVP

These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.

Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
end-to-end first, then layer on features.

---

## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)

**As a** new user,
**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
  (user is automatically logged in)
- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match
- [ ] If email is already registered, show a clear error message (do NOT reveal whether
  the email exists in the system for security -- use a generic message like "Unable to
  create account. Please check your details and try again.")
- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
- Concurrent registration with the same email
- Empty form submission
- Whitespace-only inputs
- Password exactly at minimum length

### Success Metric

- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.

---

## US-2: User Login (Must Have)

**As a** registered user,
**I want to** log in with my email and password,
**So that** I can access my personal todo list.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
- [ ] Invalid credentials show a generic error: "Invalid email or password" (do NOT
  reveal which field is wrong)
- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- User who has not registered tries to log in
- Correct email, wrong password
- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)

### Success Metric

- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.

---

## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** log out of my account,
**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Double-clicking logout
- Logout when session has already expired
- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)

### Success Metric

- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.

---

## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
**So that** I know what I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`)
- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a friendly, helpful message
  (e.g., "No todos yet. Add one above to get started.") -- not a blank screen
- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
- [ ] Page works without JavaScript

### Edge Cases

- User with zero todos (empty state)
- User with one todo
- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)

### Success Metric

- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.

---

## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
**So that** I can track a task I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo")
- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
  next item)
- [ ] Empty title submission is rejected with a clear inline error
- [ ] Title has a maximum length (TBD by team -- suggested 200 characters)
- [ ] Whitespace-only titles are rejected
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
  HTMX swap

### Edge Cases

- Adding an item with only whitespace
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has an edit mechanism (e.g., clicking the title, an edit button)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or

# User Stories -- MVP

These user stories define the MVP scope. They are conversations, not contracts -- the
whole team owns them and anyone can propose changes with good reasoning.

Priority order follows the walking skeleton approach: get the thinnest slice working
end-to-end first, then layer on features.

---

## US-1: User Registration (Must Have)

**As a** new user,
**I want to** create an account with my email and password,
**So that** I have a personal, private space to manage my todos.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] User can navigate to a registration page from the login page
- [ ] Registration form has fields: email, password, password confirmation
- [ ] **Open question for mob**: Consider replacing password confirmation with a password
  visibility toggle (show/hide). The toggle is better UX (see what you typed) but
  requires JS. Proposal: keep confirm field for no-JS baseline, add visibility toggle
  as progressive enhancement that hides the confirm field when JS is available.
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Submit button label is "Create account" (action words, not "Submit" or "Register")
- [ ] Successful registration creates a new user account and redirects to the todo list
  (user is automatically logged in)
- [ ] Email must be a valid email format
- [ ] Password must be at least 8 characters
- [ ] Password and password confirmation must match (unless mob decides on toggle approach)
- [ ] If email is already registered, show a secure, helpful error message that does NOT
  reveal whether the email exists (account enumeration prevention): "Unable to create
  account. If you already have an account, try signing in." with a link to the login page
- [ ] Validation errors are shown inline next to the relevant field
- [ ] Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] Password is hashed with Argon2 before storage (never stored in plaintext)
- [ ] Registration event is logged with structured tracing (no PII in logs)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances with inline validation and no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- Email with unusual but valid format (e.g., `user+tag@example.com`)
- Very long email or password (enforce reasonable max lengths)
- SQL injection attempts in email/password fields (parameterized queries prevent this)
- Concurrent registration with the same email
- Empty form submission
- Whitespace-only inputs
- Password exactly at minimum length

### Success Metric

- A user can register and immediately start using their todo list in under 30 seconds.

---

## US-2: User Login (Must Have)

**As a** registered user,
**I want to** log in with my email and password,
**So that** I can access my personal todo list.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Login page is the default landing page for unauthenticated users
- [ ] Login form has fields: email, password
- [ ] Every form input has a visible `<label>` element
- [ ] Successful login redirects to the user's todo list
- [ ] Invalid credentials show a generic error: "Invalid email or password" (do NOT
  reveal which field is wrong)
- [ ] Error message is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Login page has a link to the registration page
- [ ] Session is created server-side on successful login
- [ ] Failed login attempts are logged with structured tracing (no PII)
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript
- [ ] HTMX enhances with no full-page reload

### Edge Cases

- User who has not registered tries to log in
- Correct email, wrong password
- Case sensitivity in email (emails should be case-insensitive)
- Brute force attempts (rate limiting is a v2 concern, but log failed attempts)
- Session fixation (generate new session ID on login)
- User tries to access todo list URL directly without being logged in (redirect to login)

### Success Metric

- A returning user can log in and see their todo list in under 10 seconds.

---

## US-3: User Logout (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** log out of my account,
**So that** my todo list is protected when I'm done using the app.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Logout button/link is visible on every authenticated page
- [ ] Clicking logout destroys the server-side session
- [ ] After logout, user is redirected to the login page
- [ ] After logout, the back button does not show authenticated content (no cache leaks)
- [ ] Logout works without JavaScript (standard link or form submission)
- [ ] Logout event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Double-clicking logout
- Logout when session has already expired
- Logout from multiple tabs (session destroyed on server; other tabs redirect on next request)

### Success Metric

- User can log out with a single click and is confident their session is ended.

---

## US-4: View Todo List (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** see my todo list when I log in,
**So that** I know what I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Todo list is the main screen after login
- [ ] Page has a clear heading (e.g., "My Todos") as the `<h1>`
- [ ] Todo items are displayed in a semantic list (`<ul>` or `<ol>`)
- [ ] Each item shows its title and completion status
- [ ] Completed items are visually distinct from pending items (not just color --
  a secondary indicator like strikethrough text)
- [ ] **Empty state**: When the user has no todos, show a friendly, helpful message
  (e.g., "No todos yet. Add one above to get started.") -- not a blank screen
- [ ] Page title (`<title>`) is descriptive (e.g., "My Todos -- Todo List")
- [ ] The page includes proper landmark regions (`<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`)
- [ ] Skip link to main content is the first focusable element
- [ ] Each user can only see their own todos (authorization check)
- [ ] Page works without JavaScript

### Edge Cases

- User with zero todos (empty state)
- User with one todo
- User with many todos (100+) -- still performant and usable
- Todo titles with special characters, HTML entities, or very long text
- Accessing another user's todo list URL (must return 403 or redirect)

### Success Metric

- A user can see their entire todo list within 1 second of logging in. The list is
  scannable -- the user's eye goes directly to what needs doing.

---

## US-5: Add a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** add a new todo item to my list,
**So that** I can track a task I need to do.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Input field for the todo title is prominently placed (above or at the top of the list)
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (e.g., "New todo")
- [ ] Submitting the form adds the item to the list immediately
- [ ] New items are added in the `Pending` state
- [ ] After adding, the input field is cleared and focus returns to it (ready for the
  next item)
- [ ] Empty title submission is rejected with a clear inline error
- [ ] Title has a maximum length (TBD by team -- suggested 200 characters)
- [ ] Whitespace-only titles are rejected
- [ ] Form works without JavaScript (full page reload, item appears in list)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is appended to the list without page reload
- [ ] New item creation is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] `aria-live` region announces "Todo added" (or similar) to screen readers after
  HTMX swap

### Edge Cases

- Adding an item with only whitespace
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has an edit mechanism (e.g., clicking the title, an edit button)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
  focus returns to the item
- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
- Very long replacement text
- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)

### Success Metric

- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

---

## Priority and Implementation Order

Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:

1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)

This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
8-10 are enhancements on the core.

---

## Product Success Criteria (MVP)

The MVP is successful when:

1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD

We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.

- Adding an item with only whitespace
- Adding an item with very long text (at or beyond max length)
- Adding an item with special characters, HTML, or emoji
- Rapid successive additions (no duplicate submissions)
- Adding when disconnected (graceful failure)

### Success Metric

- Adding a todo takes fewer than 5 seconds from thought to captured item. The flow
  feels as fast as jotting something on a sticky note.

---

## US-6: Complete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** mark a todo item as complete,
**So that** I can see what I've accomplished and focus on what's left.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each pending todo item has a mechanism to mark it complete (e.g., a checkbox or button)
- [ ] The mechanism is keyboard accessible
- [ ] Completing an item visually updates it immediately (e.g., strikethrough, dimmed,
  moved to a "completed" section)
- [ ] Completed items remain visible in the list (not hidden)
- [ ] A visual indicator beyond color alone marks completed items (e.g., strikethrough + check)
- [ ] Completion can be undone (mark as pending again) -- toggling state
- [ ] State change works without JavaScript (form submission)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: in-place update without page reload
- [ ] State change is announced to screen readers via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Completion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Focus remains on or near the toggled item after the update

### Edge Cases

- Completing an already-completed item (toggle back to pending)
- Completing an item that was deleted by another tab (graceful error)
- Rapid toggling (no race conditions)

### Success Metric

- Completing a todo is a single action (one click or keypress). The visual feedback
  is immediate and satisfying.

---

## US-7: Delete a Todo Item (Must Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** delete a todo item I no longer need,
**So that** my list stays clean and focused.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has a delete action (button, not a link -- this is a destructive action)
- [ ] Delete button has an accessible label (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries")
- [ ] Deletion removes the item from the list immediately
- [ ] Deletion is permanent (no undo in MVP -- but confirm before deleting)
- [ ] Confirmation mechanism before deletion (e.g., "Are you sure?" or visually distinct
  confirm/cancel) -- keep it lightweight, not a modal dialog
- [ ] After deletion, focus moves to the next item in the list (or to the "add" input
  if the list is now empty)
- [ ] Deletion works without JavaScript (form submission, page reload)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: item is removed from DOM without page reload
- [ ] Screen reader announcement: "Todo deleted" via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Deletion event is logged with structured tracing
- [ ] Deleting someone else's todo is not possible (authorization)

### Edge Cases

- Deleting the last item in the list (transitions to empty state)
- Deleting an item that was already deleted (e.g., in another tab)
- Confirmation dismissed (item should not be deleted)

### Success Metric

- Deleting a todo is quick but safe. Users never accidentally lose a todo item.

---

## US-8: Edit a Todo Item Title (Should Have)

**As a** logged-in user,
**I want to** edit the title of an existing todo item,
**So that** I can fix typos or update the description without deleting and re-creating.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each todo item has an edit mechanism (e.g., clicking the title, an edit button)
- [ ] Edit mode replaces the title with an input field pre-filled with the current title
- [ ] Input has a visible `<label>` (may be visually hidden if the context is clear,
  but must be present for screen readers)
- [ ] Save and cancel actions are available (e.g., Enter to save, Escape to cancel, or
  explicit Save/Cancel buttons)
- [ ] Same validation as adding: non-empty, max length, no whitespace-only
- [ ] Validation errors shown inline
- [ ] After saving, the item returns to display mode with the updated title
- [ ] After canceling, the original title is restored
- [ ] Edit works without JavaScript (navigate to an edit page/form, save redirects back)
- [ ] HTMX enhances: inline editing without page navigation
- [ ] Focus management: on entering edit mode, focus moves to the input; on saving/canceling,
  focus returns to the item
- [ ] Screen reader: state change announced via `aria-live` region
- [ ] Edit event is logged with structured tracing

### Edge Cases

- Editing to an empty title (validation error)
- Editing while another tab has deleted the item
- Editing a completed item (should work the same way)
- Very long replacement text
- Pressing Escape after making changes (discard changes)
- Saving with no changes (no-op, no error)

### Success Metric

- Fixing a typo in a todo item takes under 5 seconds. The inline editing feels natural
  and doesn't interrupt the user's flow.

---

## Priority and Implementation Order

Following the walking skeleton principle, the implementation order is:

1. **Project scaffolding** (Task #1) -- Cargo.toml, project structure, docker-compose, config
2. **Walking skeleton** (Task #2) -- Health check endpoint with integration test
3. **Design tokens** (Task #3) -- CSS custom properties foundation
4. **US-1: Registration** (Task #4) -- First real feature, proves the full stack works
5. **US-2: Login** (Task #5) -- Requires registration; completes the auth flow
6. **US-3: Logout** (Task #5) -- Bundled with login (same session management)
7. **US-4: View todo list + US-5: Add todo** (Task #6) -- Core loop: see list, add items
8. **US-6: Complete todo** (Task #7) -- State change
9. **US-7: Delete todo** (Task #8) -- Removal
10. **US-8: Edit todo title** (Task #9) -- Polish (should have)

This order delivers value incrementally. After step 7, we have a usable product. Steps
8-10 are enhancements on the core.

---

## Product Success Criteria (MVP)

The MVP is successful when:

1. A new user can sign up, log in, manage todos (add, complete, delete), and log out
2. The experience loads fast (< 1 second for any page)
3. The app is fully accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA)
4. The app works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
5. The architecture cleanly supports future iteration
6. The codebase has comprehensive test coverage via TDD

We will know this is working when a first-time user can accomplish the complete
add-complete-delete flow without hesitation or confusion.

# Future Ideas (v2+)

Ideas the team loves but that are **out of scope for MVP**. We capture them here so
they don't get lost -- and so we stop talking about them during the current iteration.

The answer to "Wouldn't it be cool if..." is **"Yes -- in v2."**

---

## Feature Ideas

| Idea | Who Raised It | Why It's Interesting | Why Not Now |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Multiple lists / categories | -- | Users may want to organize by context | MVP validates single-list usage first |
| Due dates and reminders | -- | Time-sensitive tasks need deadlines | Adds calendar complexity; validate core loop first |
| Sharing / collaboration | -- | Shared grocery lists, family tasks | Multi-user interaction is a different product |
| Tags or labels | -- | Filtering and organization | Validate that a flat list is sufficient first |
| Search and filter | -- | Finding items in long lists | MVP lists are expected to be short |
| Mobile app (native) | -- | Better mobile experience | Responsive web is sufficient for MVP |
| Social login (OAuth) | -- | Easier signup flow | Email/password is simpler to implement and test |
| Dark mode | -- | User preference, reduced eye strain | Can be added later with CSS custom property reassignment |
| Reorder via drag-and-drop | -- | Nice UX for prioritization | "Could have" in MVP; defer if it delays launch |
| Undo/redo | -- | Safety net for accidental actions | Adds state complexity; confirm dialogs suffice for MVP |
| Recurring tasks | -- | Daily/weekly habits | Different mental model; validate one-off tasks first |
| Subtasks / checklists | -- | Breaking down complex tasks | Adds hierarchy; flat list validates the core need |

---

## How to Add Ideas

When someone on the team (or a user) suggests a feature beyond MVP scope:

1. Add it to the table above
2. Note who raised it and why it's interesting
3. Explain briefly why it's deferred
4. Move on -- don't design it, don't discuss implementation details

content.text: Good. Now let me raise the password confirmation question to the mob since it needs broader input.

input.content:
Heydon, I need your accessibility perspective on a UX question for registration (US-1).

Steve Krug is proposing we drop the "confirm password" field and replace it with a password visibility toggle (show/hide button). His argument: every extra field is a reason to abandon the form.

My concern is progressive enhancement. The visibility toggle requires JavaScript. Without JS:
- With a confirm field: user types password twice, catches typos. Accessible, works everywhere.
- Without a confirm field and no toggle: user types password once into a masked field with no way to verify what they typed.

My proposal: Keep the confirm field as the no-JS baseline. When JS is available, add a visibility toggle and hide the confirm field (progressive enhancement).

Questions for you:
1. From an accessibility standpoint, is a password visibility toggle well-supported by screen readers? (I'm thinking `<button>` with aria-label toggling between "Show password" / "Hide password", and the input type toggling between password/text.)
2. Any WCAG concerns with either approach?
3. Do you have a preference?

This will come up when we build Task #4 (registration). Want to get your input early.

content.text:
User stories are updated. Here's a summary of what I just did:

**Updated in `docs/user-stories.md`:**

1. **US-1 (Registration)**: Added "Create account" as the button label. Flagged the password confirm vs. visibility toggle as an open question for the mob. Tightened the duplicate-email error copy to be both secure (no account enumeration) and helpful ("If you already have an account, try signing in." with a link).

2. **US-2 (Login)**: Added "Sign in" as the button label. Softened the error copy to "That email or password didn't work. Try again." Added a "Forgot password?" placeholder link acceptance criterion -- the link exists for MVP but leads to a "coming soon" page (password reset infrastructure is out of scope).

3. **US-5 (Add todo)**: Added placeholder text suggestion "What do you need to do?" to the input field criterion.

**Updated in `docs/future-ideas.md`:**
- Added "Undo delete (toast-style)" -- credited to Steve Krug
- Added "Password reset (forgot password)" -- credited to Steve Krug
- Split out "Undo/redo (general)" as a separate idea

**Sent to Heydon Pickering**: Asked for his accessibility take on the password confirm vs. visibility toggle question before the mob starts building registration.

**Held the line on:**
- No account enumeration in error messages (security over friendliness)
- Inline confirm for delete rather than undo (undo goes to future ideas)
- Progressive enhancement requirement for any JS-dependent feature
